coredata.dc:title: Magnetically separable and recyclable ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles as an effective activator in resorcinol-formaldehyde resins-based photocatalysis-self-Fenton system

coredata.dc:description:
Non-homogeneous photocatalysis-self-Fenton technology presents an effective strategy for efficient photocatalytic degradation of pollutants, circumventing the issues of extensive contamination of water resources by free iron ions and reducing safety risks and cost issues associated with the generation of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). In the photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction, the activation efficiency of H2O2 determines the capacity for photocatalytic degradation and mineralization of pollutants. Consequently, this study adopted ZnFe2O4 (ZFO) as an efficient, magnetically recoverable H2O2 activator, successfully coupling it with resorcinol–formaldehyde (RF) resin to construct a photocatalysis-self-Fenton system, thereby achieving degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible light. The experimental results revealed that apparent rate constant (k app) for the RF/ZFO photocatalysis-self-Fenton system is 22.997 times higher than that of the Fenton system and 1.156 times higher than that of the photo-Fenton system. Under visible light, the RF resin can generate H2O2 in situ and is subsequently activated by ZFO, resulting in a highly oxidized hydroxyl radical (
OH), attacking contaminants. This study presents new insights into the construction of efficient heterogeneous radiofrequency resin photocatalysis-self-Fenton degradation system and provides new solutions for efficient and environmentally friendly wastewater treatment.

head.ce:title: Magnetically separable and recyclable ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles as an effective activator in resorcinol-formaldehyde resins-based photocatalysis-self-Fenton system

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Non-homogeneous photocatalysis-self-Fenton technology presents an effective strategy for efficient photocatalytic degradation of pollutants, circumventing the issues of extensive contamination of water resources by free iron ions and reducing safety risks and cost issues associated with the generation of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). In the photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction, the activation efficiency of H2O2 determines the capacity for photocatalytic degradation and mineralization of pollutants. Consequently, this study adopted ZnFe2O4 (ZFO) as an efficient, magnetically recoverable H2O2 activator, successfully coupling it with resorcinol–formaldehyde (RF) resin to construct a photocatalysis-self-Fenton system, thereby achieving degradation of tetracycline (TC) under visible light. The experimental results revealed that apparent rate constant (k app) for the RF/ZFO photocatalysis-self-Fenton system is 22.997 times higher than that of the Fenton system and 1.156 times higher than that of the photo-Fenton system. Under visible light, the RF resin can generate H2O2 in situ and is subsequently activated by ZFO, resulting in a highly oxidized hydroxyl radical (
OH), attacking contaminants. This study presents new insights into the construction of efficient heterogeneous radiofrequency resin photocatalysis-self-Fenton degradation system and provides new solutions for efficient and environmentally friendly wastewater treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics are widely applied in fauna and flora, with their presence routinely detected in diverse environments such as hospital wastewater and agricultural soils, which poses significant impediments to resolving environmental issues and sustaining human health [1]. We know that the environment is the cornerstone of human survival and evolution, so the development of cost-effective environmental purification antibiotic technology is considerable urgent [2]. In this context, semiconductor-based photocatalysis with low cost, simple operation and high stability has been widely anticipated as a promising solution to water pollution [3]. Regrettably, the capacity of a solitary photocatalysis to mineralize pollutants is relatively deficient. Consequently, a prospective approach involves coupling photocatalysis with other advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) to achieve superior synergistic effects [4], the amalgamation of the two far surpasses the sum of their individual impacts, unveiling boundless possibilities.

Recently, the advent of photocatalysis-self-Fenton technology as the times require, a hybrid of photocatalysis and Fenton technology, ingeniously utilizes in-situ generated H2O2 from photocatalysis as a Fenton reagent [5], which circumvents the need for the copious addition of H2O2 required in traditional Fenton and photo-Fenton reactions [6,7], significantly reducing the cost of producing H2O2 and the associated risks during transportation [8,9]. The H2O2 generated in-situ via photocatalysis is activated through the Fenton reaction, producing a plethora of hydroxyl radicals (
OH) that facilitate efficient pollutant removal and mineralization, which promotes the sustained progression of the Fenton reaction. However, the issue of free-state iron ions dispersing throughout the solution following the homogeneous photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction typically poses a significant challenge, as they are difficult to separate and recover [10], which can easily lead to the formation of iron sludge, severely impeding the reuse of treated water resources. Consequently, employing non-homogeneous iron sources as Fenton reagents emerges as a critical choice for the eco-friendly transformation of wastewater into potable water, facilitating the reuse of water resource’s post-reaction. In non-homogeneous photocatalysis-self-Fenton reactions, the utilization efficiency of H2O2 significantly influences the photocatalytic degradation performance [11,12]. Therefore, it is imperative to select a suitable iron source as an efficient H2O2 activator to promote the abundant generation of active species for exhibiting superior degradation performance.

Among the plethora of suitable solid iron sources, ZnFe2O4 (ZFO) has garnered widespread attention due to its excellent photochemical stability, cost-effectiveness, and significant visible light absorption capability as a magnetically recoverable and iron-rich spinel ferrite [13–18]. Previous reports have been confirmed the ability of ZFO to efficiently activate H2O2 and subsequently generate reactive species [19], making ZFO a rational choice as an effective H2O2 activator to facilitate the efficient progression of the photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction. Concurrently, the use of magnetic heterogeneous iron sources not only plays its inherent role but also benefits recycling and reuse, significantly mitigating the impact on water resources and aiding in the establishment of an environmentally friendly photocatalysis-self-Fenton system. In the quest for an ideal photocatalysis-self-Fenton system, a photocatalyst capable of in situ generation of an optimal amount of H2O2 is fundamental to the system's assembly. Resorcinol-formaldehyde (RF) resin, known for its low cost, straightforward synthesis process, and high stability as a cross-linked polymer, is considered an excellent choice for H2O2 production [20,21]. Therefore, coupling ZFO with RF resin, utilizing ZFO as an efficient H2O2 activator and RF resin as the source for in situ generation of H2O2, constructs an efficient photocatalysis-self-Fenton system with magnetically separable and recyclable properties, representing a prime selection for eco-friendly environmental treatment strategies.

In this work, a RF resin-based photocatalysis-self-Fenton system employing ZFO as an efficient H2O2 activator is constructed, exhibits superior photocatalytic degradation and mineralization performance for TC solution without the need of additional H2O2 under visible light irradiation, surpassing both Fenton and photo-Fenton systems. The cyclic transformation of Fe3+/Fe2+ and the utilization of H2O2 in various systems were investigated, and the potential mechanism and possible mineralization pathways of the RF/ZFO photocatalysis-self-Fenton degradation system were discussed in detail.

Resorcinol (C6H6O2, AR), formaldehyde solution (CH2O, 37 wt%), ammonium hydroxide solution (NH3
H2O, 28 wt%–30 wt%), Fe(NO3)3
9H2O, Zn(NO3)2
6H2O Hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB) and NaOH were provided from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. All the materials are of analytical grade and used without further purification.

Resorcinol-formaldehyde (RF) resins nanospheres were prepared utilizing a one-step hydrothermal method based on previous report [22]. In brief, 792 mg of resorcinol, 1080 μL of formaldehyde solution and 460 μL of NH3
H2O solution were added to 60 mL of deionized water, a white colloidal suspension was emergence by stirring at ambient temperature for 10 min. Then, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 250 °C for 24 h. After cooling to room temperature, the precipitate was washed with deionized water and ethanol. The reddish-brown powder was obtained after drying under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h.

In short, 35 mL of Zn(NO3)2 solution (0.2 M), 35 mL of Fe(NO3)3 solution (0.4 M) and 1.0 g CTAB were stirred for 30 min. Subsequently, by the dropwise addition of NaOH solution (0.1 M), until the pH reached a value of 10, and the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 130 °C for 24 h. The obtained precipitate was washed with deionized water and ethanol after cooling the solution to room temperature and the brick red ZnFe2O4 (ZFO) powder was collected after drying under vacuum at 60 °C for 12 h.

Photocatalytic-self-Fenton degradation system with RF as the H2O2 photocatalyst and ZFO as the activating agent can be constructed and the corresponding diagrammatic sketch is given in Scheme 1 , and the degradation efficiency of antibiotic (tetracycline, TC) during RF/ZFO photocatalytic-self-Fenton degradation system the was evaluated in visible light using a multi-channel photoreactor (Beijing Pefectlight PCX-50C). During the photocatalysis-self-Fenton degradation experiment, 25 mg of RF with different masses of ZFO (m = 1, 3, 5, 7 and 10 mg) were first added to 50 mL of a given concentration of antibiotic solution (20 mg/L TC), then stirred in the dark for 30 min to reach adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Afterwards, the light source was turned on during the experiment process. Based on a certain time intervals, 2 mL of suspension was taken from TC solution and measured at the characteristic absorption peak wavelength of 357 nm with a UV–Vis spectrophotometer for evaluating the degradation performance.
A series of material characterization, photocatalytic H2O2 production and electrochemical tests were listed in the Supporting Information .

The crystal structures of the synthesized RF and ZFO were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, as depicted in Fig. 1 a. The XRD pattern of RF at 2θ ≈ 23° reveals a broad diffraction peak corresponding to the graphite carbon (002) plane, attributable to π-stacking induced by the stacking of electron donor and acceptor units [23]. For the XRD pattern of ZFO, the peaks at 2θ = 30.1, 35.2, 42.8, 56.5, and 67.8° precisely delineates the characteristic diffraction peaks of the ZnFe2O4 cubic spinel structure (JPCDS 01-1109) at the (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440) planes respectively [13]. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of RF and ZFO are presented in Fig. 1b. The FT-IR spectrum of RF resin distinctly exhibits unique bands originating from resorcinol, linkers, and residual groups with the characteristic peaks, corresponding to υO–H vibrations of resorcinol or methylol are observed at 3353 cm−1, υC–H vibrations of methylene linkers at 2921 cm−1, υC=C vibrations of aromatic rings at 1610 cm−1, and υC-O of resorcinol and methylol at 1096 cm−1, respectively [8]. In the case of ZFO, the FT-IR spectrum reveals characteristic peaks at 3356, 528, and 415 cm−1, which are associated with O–H bending due to adsorbed H2O, as well as stretching vibrations of Zn-O bonds at the tetrahedral and Fe-O bonds at the octahedral positions, respectively [19]. The morphology of RF and ZFO were investigated using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1c and Fig. S1a illustrate that RF resin is spherical with an average diameter of approximately 500 nm and a uniform size distribution, while Fig. 1d and Fig. S1b reveal that ZFO nanoparticles demonstrating spherical configurations of less than 10 nm in diameter. Furthermore, elemental mapping images and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) analysis have confirmed the uniform distribution of various elements within the RF resin spheres (Fig. 1e), as well as within the ZFO nanoparticles (Fig. 1f and Fig.S2). The presence of iron in ZFO, along with its high concentration, constitutes a favorable premise for developing ZFO as an efficient activator for photocatalysis-self-Fenton reactions.

Within the photocatalysis-self-Fenton system constituted by RF resin and ZFO, RF boasts the ability to efficiently photocatalytic production of H2O2 under visible light, providing a foundational prerequisite for the construction of the RF resin-based photocatalysis-self-Fenton degradation system. Initially, RF resin triggers the generation of H2O2 under visible light, subsequently ZFO operates as an efficient Fenton activator, accomplishes the efficient activation of in-situ photocatalysis for H2O2 generation, and as a result produces an abundant number of active species such as hydroxyl radicals (
OH), revealing potent photocatalysis pollutant degradation capabilities. An in-depth exploration into the reasons for RF resin possessing the ability to photocatalytic production of H2O2 was conducted. The optical absorption feature of RF resin was investigated utilizing ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), as depicted in Fig.S3, the RF resin exhibited superior visible light response capability due to the charge transfer (CT) transition of electron donor–acceptor (D-A) couples [24]. Moreover, the band gap (Eg ) value of RF resin, calculated through the Kubelka-Munk formula stands at 2.1 eV (Fig.S4). The type and flat-band potentials (E fb) of RF resin (Fig.S5) was measured utilizing Mott-Schottky (M−S), where RF resin exhibited a positive slope characteristic of n-type semiconductors [25], indicating the generation of a low-bandgap semiconductor due to CT transition of the π-stacking hybridized D-A couples [26], with its energy band position illustrated in Fig.S6. The Efb values for RF resin is −0.553 eV (vs. Ag/AgCl), which is corresponded to −0.36 eV versus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE, ENHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.197 eV). Consequently, based on the equation Eg = EVB − ECB , the valence band (VB) potential (E VB) of RF can be estimated as 1.74 eV. This suitable energy band structure provides an adequate theoretical foundation, indicating that RF resin could effectively generate H2O2 via photocatalysis. The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra of H2O2 (Fig.S7) and concentration test vouched for the ability of RF resin to efficient photocatalytic generate H2O2. As depicted in Fig. 2 a, according to the standard curve of photocatalytic production of H2O2 (Fig.S8), RF resin can generate 470 μM of H2O2 through photocatalysis under 2 h of visible light irradiation. Upon introduction of ZFO, the mixed system displayed a declining trend in the performance of photocatalytic H2O2 production, implying that ZFO, acting as an iron source, initiates the Fenton reaction activating the H2O2 to produce a profusion of active species (Eq. (1)). In the process of photocatalytic production of H2O2, it is typically accompanied by two reactions, specifically, the 2 e- water oxidation reaction (WOR) and 2 e- oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) [27,28]. Within the two-electron oxygen reduction reaction, there is the direct two-electron ORR reaction (Eq. (2)) and two-step single-electron ORR reactions (Eqs. (3) and (4)) [29,30]. Consequently, by altering environmental conditions, investigations into the process of RF resin photocatalytic production of H2O2 took place, as shown in Fig. 2b. Under the N2 environment, only trace amounts of H2O2 were detected, and combining with the environment of air and without external inflation, it can be inferred that the main pathway for RF resin photocatalytic production of H2O2 is through the ORR reaction, indicating that the dissolved oxygen in the reaction conditions plays a pivotal role in the process of RF resin photocatalytic production of H2O2. During this process,
O2 – acts as a key intermediary product in the two-step single-electron ORR reaction, the variation in signal peaks in ESR spectra (Fig.S9) of DMPO-
O2 – after light initiation further indicates the presence of
O2 –. Concurrently,
O2 – also participates in the Photo-Fenton degradation reaction, contributing to the efficient degradation of antibiotics [31]. Within the photocatalysis-self-Fenton degradation system, simultaneous in-situ photocatalytic production and decomposition of H2O2 occur concurrently. The test results for H2O2 decomposition by RF resin and ZFO (Fig. 2c) reveals that RF exhibits a relatively low H2O2 decomposition rate, which helps RF resin photocatalytic produce high concentrations of H2O2. In contrast, ZFO exhibits a higher H2O2 decomposition rate, which assists in the high-efficiency activation and utilization of H2O2, providing practical substantiation for ZFO as an effective H2O2 activating agent. By contrasting the photocatalytic degradation performance of 20 mg/L TC solutions under Fenton system (F), Photo-Fenton system (PF), and RF/ZFO photocatalysis-self-Fenton system (PSF), as illustrated in Fig. 2d and Fig.S10, it is quite apparent that the PSF degradation system emerges with a supremacy position. Specifically, the degradation effect of the RF resin-based PSF degradation system incorporating ZFO as an efficient activator reached 67.3 % on a 20 mg/L TC solution, this degradation performance surpasses the 57.9 % of the PF system constructed with ZFO and 470 μM H2O2 under visible light irradiation, and significantly outperforms the 15.7 % degradation by the Fenton system constituted by ZFO and 470 μM H2O2. Furthermore, the apparent rate constant (k) value (Fig. 2e) of the RF resin-based PSF degradation system with ZFO acting as an efficient activator was significantly higher than that of the PF system by 1.156 times and the Fenton system by 22.997 times, respectively, demonstrating the enhancement of photocatalytic degradation performance brought about by the RF resin-based PSF degradation system featuring ZFO as an efficient activator relative to other systems. The constructed RF/ZFO photocatalysis-self-Fenton system is comparable to the photocatalytic and photo-Fenton activities reported in recent years (Table S1). The improvement in the performance is attributed to the PSF degradation reaction, wherein Fe3+ combines with photo-generated electrons resulting in the production of Fe2+ (Eq. (5)), which accelerates the redox cyclic transformation of the iron species in the Fe3+/Fe2+ state (Fig. 2f) [32], effectively leveraging the iron source which in turn efficiently transforms H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals (
OH), thereby enhancing the photocatalytic degradation ability for the effective removal of antibiotic pollutants. Moreover, compared to the PF system, the PSF system can utilize the H2O2 produced in-situ by photocatalysis, with effective activation by the iron species, the utilization rate of H2O2 significantly increases, consequently enhancing the performance of photocatalytic degradation. In the RF/ZFO PSF degradation system, the photocatalytic performance is the highest elevation when the added amount of ZFO is 5 mg (Fig. 2g). When the concentration of ZFO, as the iron source activator, is too low, effective activation of H2O2 cannot be carried out, thereby demonstrating the lower enhancement in photocatalytic degradation activity, while when the concentration of ZFO, as the iron source activator, is too high, it leads to substantial Fe3+ reacting with photo-generated electrons, thereby inhibiting the process of photocatalytic generation of H2O2 [33]. The effect of fluctuations in the pH ranges and its effects on the performance of the RF/ZFO PSF degradation system are discussed (Fig. 2h). It is well known that the performance of the Fenton reaction is optimal in acidic circumstances where the decomposition rate of H2O2 is swift and the generation rate of
OH is elevated, thereby exhibits superior degradation performance [34]. However, when the pH value is excessively high, iron ions tend to form ferric hydroxide complex, leading to a decreased photocatalytic activity [10]. Remarkably, the constructed RF/ZFO photocatalysis-self-Fenton system shows an excellent ability to degrade pollutants in neutral and slightly alkaline wastewater. Additionally, the impact of different ions on the photocatalytic self-Fenton degradation process is considered to be minimal (Fig.S11). Therefore, the perfect fusion of various conditions such as apt environmental reaction conditions is required for an efficient PSF system. Moreover, the stability of photocatalytic materials plays a pivotal role in practical applications. Following four consecutive cycle tests, it is discernible that the RF/ZFO PSF degradation system demonstrates a persistent photocatalytic degradation activity towards TC solution (Fig. 2i). Analyzing the variations in transmission electron microscopy images (Fig.S12) and XRD patterns (Fig.S13) before and after the reaction, reveals that the microscopic morphological structure of the photocatalytic material remains unchanged [35,36], thereby emphatically attesting to the robust cyclic stability of the RF resin-based photocatalysis-self-Fenton degradation system with ZFO as an effective activator.
(1) Fe2+ + H2O2 → Fe3++ OH– +
OH
(2) O2 + 2e- + 2H+ → H2O2
(3) O2 + e- →
O2 –
(4)
O2 – + e- + 2H+ → H2O2
(5) Fe3+ + e− → Fe2+

As displayed in Fig. 3 a and Video S1, by applying a magnet to achieve solid–liquid separation of an aqueous solution containing ZFO, the ZFO manifested magnetically recoverable properties. With the attraction of the magnet, the ZFO in the aqueous solution was clarified from the suspension, demonstrating a stratified phenomenon with the aggregation of ZFO. The change in the oxidation state of the Fe element of solid Fenton reagent ZFO during the PSF degradation reaction process was assessed to delve into the transforming details of the iron source's valence state, as illustrated in Fig. 3b. Initially, the ratio of Fe3+/Fe2+ stood at 0.72, elevating to 0.98 after the reaction. Clearly, a change in the valence state of iron elements in ZFO occurred after the PSF degradation reaction, and the relative increase of Fe3+ implies the onset of the Fenton reaction, thereby producing an abundance of strongly oxidative
OH radicals. Consequently, the real-time ESR radical detection of the two components in the reaction system were performed. The signal corresponding to DMPO-
OH of the RF resin showed an increasing trend over time (Fig. 3c), this change was relatively minor. In contrast, when ZFO was integrated as a solid Fenton reagent with RF resin, the signal for DMPO-
OH observed a significant increase in the PSF system (Fig. 3d), which indicates a substantial enhancement in the content of
OH radicals following the construction of PSF degradation system. The concentration of Fe2+ throughout the PSF reaction process (Fig.S14) revealed a clear trend of prominent decrease to gradually stabilizing of Fe2+ concentration, indicative of the inherent intricate oxidation–reduction interactions occurring during photocatalysis. By contrast, an independent Fenton reaction demonstrated consistently decrease in Fe2+ concentrations. Amid the dynamic reaction transformations, Fe3+ combines with photo-generated electrons to produce Fe2+ (Eq. (5)), supplementing the original Fenton reagent and enabling the continuous PSF degradation reaction. Utilizing ZFO as a solid Fenton reagent substantially reduces the contamination of water resources with free-state iron ions by mitigating the spillage of iron ions. Moreover, the fixed Fe species adeptly maintain the cyclical interaction of Fe3+/Fe2+, rendering the post-reaction water resources an eco-friendly choice. Another contributing factor to the enhanced photocatalytic performance of the constructed PSF system is its efficient utilization of H2O2, ascertained by measuring the concentration of ∙OH, which using fluorescence spectroscopy of coumarin and ∙OH to produce 7-hydroxycoumarin (Fig.S15) [37]. It can be observed from Fig. 3e that with increasing irradiation duration, the concentration of
OH radicals in the PSF system consistently grows, reaching heights exceeding alternative systems. In contrast, the concentration of
OH in the Fenton reaction ceased production over time, a concordant result with the degradation effect of different systems on TC solution (Fig. 2d). Such results signify that the PSF system exhibits a dramatically superior H2O2 utilization rate compared to other systems (Fig. 3f), noteworthy is the fact that the efficiency of the utilization of H2O2 in the PSF system is 41.29 times that of the Fenton system, and 1.33 times greater than that of the photo-Fenton system. This result certifies why the photocatalysis-self-Fenton system manifests superior photocatalytic degradation performance. Capture experiments were conducted to investigate the effect of various active substances on the degradation of the TC solution within the RF/ZFO PSF system. L-ascorbic acid (VC), tert‑butanol (TBA), L-tryptophan (LTP), and triethanolamine (TEOA) were added as agents to capture superoxide radicals (
O2 –), hydroxyl radicals (
OH), singlet oxygen (1O2) and holes (h+ ), respectively. As depicted in Fig.S16, the inclusion of TEOA throughout the entire PSF degradation experiment resulted in a slight reduction in the degradation rate of the TC solution, declining from 67.3 % to 62.7 %, indicating that h+ are not the main active species. The introduction of LTP resulted in a decrease in degradation efficiency to 32.4 %, which amply demonstrates the concerted contribution of the non-radical 1O₂ degradation system. The synergistic action of radicals and non-radicals facilitates the enhancement of the RF/ZFO PSF system's degradation performance. Upon the introduction of TBA and VC into the reaction process led to a substantial decrease in the degradation rates of the TC solution, plummeting to 26.1 % and 13 % respectively, and the outcome signifies that
O2 – and
OH play principal roles throughout the entirety of the PSF degradation reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: TOC removal rates and degradation pathways
ce:section.ce:para: Total organic carbon (TOC) is a crucial benchmark for evaluating the degree of organic pollution in water. Thus, the mineralization rates of different systems were tested, the TOC of the Fenton system (Fig. 4 a) and the PF system (Fig. 4b) achieved merely 5 % and 23.6 % respectively. Conversely, with ZFO serving as the F activator, the mineralization rate of the PSF system (Fig. 4c) reached 38.7 %, being 7.74 and 1.64 times that of the F and PF systems correspondingly. Next, liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) was employed to study the primary intermediate products (Fig.S17, 18 and 19) formed during the degradation process of TC solution in each system and to propose the plausible degradation pathways of TC solution in the constructed systems. In the Fenton system (Fig. 4d), the primary intermediates identified are characterized by m/z values of 417, 340, 242, 427, 413, 256, 60, 98, and 118, as a result, two main degradation pathways are proposed. Pathway Ⅰ involves the demethylation of TC to form P1 (m/z = 417), which is subsequently attacked by active species leading to deacetylation and the formation of P2 (m/z = 340) and further ring-opening to form P3 (m/z = 242) [38,39]. In Pathway Ⅱ, TC molecules are oxidized through hydroxylation by active species attacks to form P4 (m/z = 427), followed by demethylation to generate P5 (m/z = 413), and then oxidative ring cleavage to yield P6 (m/z = 256) [40]. Both pathways ultimately lead to the formation of low molecular weight compounds (P7, P8, P9, and P10) that are mineralized into inorganic substances such as CO2 and H2O. In the photo-Fenton system (Fig. 4e), three potential degradation pathways are identified. In Pathway Ⅰ, TC molecules undergo two demethylation reactions to form P1 (m/z = 418), followed by demethylation and deamidation under active species attack to produce P2 (m/z = 340), then dehydroxylation and ring-opening to yield P3 (m/z = 300), and dehydroxylation and oxidation to form P4 (m/z = 227) [41]. In Pathway Ⅱ, TC molecules are demethylated to form P5 (m/z = 415), which undergoes hydroxylation and deamidation to yield P6 (m/z = 329), followed by benzene ring cleavage and additional functional group loss to produce P7 (m/z = 239), and further demethylation and dehydroxylation to generate P8 (m/z = 151) [42,43]. In Pathway Ⅲ, TC molecules are initially demethylated to form P9 (m/z = 403), then undergo dehydroxylation and deamidation to form P10 (m/z = 326), P10 undergoes ring-opening cleavage to form P11 (m/z = 304), and further dehydroxylation to produce P12 (m/z = 283) [38,44,45]. Ultimately, the intermediates from the three pathways (P4, P8 and P12) undergo benzene ring cleavage and additional functional group loss to form even smaller molecules such as P13 (m/z = 60), P14 (m/z = 114), P15 (m/z = 118), and P16 (m/z = 195). These smaller molecules are eventually mineralized into inorganic substances. In the photocatalysis-self-Fenton system (Fig. 4f), the degradation pathways may include the following three. In pathway Ⅰ, TC molecules are attacked by active groups leading to removal of methyl groups to form the intermediate P1 (m/z = 418), followed by dehydroxylation and deamidation to form P2 (m/z = 326), and further by aldehyde and hydroxyl group removal to yield P3 (m/z = 227), followed by benzene ring cleavage, dehydroxylation and demethylation to form P4 (m/z = 146) [46–48]. In Pathway Ⅱ, TC molecules are demethylated to form P5 (m/z = 434), then the methyl on the tetracycline ring is attacked by active species to undergo a second demethylation to form P6 (m/z = 417), followed by deamidation and dehydroxylation to yield P7 (m/z = 340), and further benzene ring cleavage to produce P8 (m/z = 283) [42,49,50]. In Pathway Ⅲ, TC molecules undergo demethylation to generate P9 (m/z = 415), then dehydroxylation, epoxidation cleavage, and deamidation to form P10 (m/z = 300), and further aldehyde group removal to form P11 (m/z = 260), which then undergoes ring cleavage and demethylation to form P12 (m/z = 218) [41,51]. Ultimately, these intermediates are further transformed into smaller molecules such as P13 (m/z = 60), P14 (m/z = 74), P15 (m/z = 109), and P16 (m/z = 114) through oxidation, ring-opening reactions, and demethylation. These intermediates are eventually mineralized into inorganic substances like CO2 and H2O. Observation of the aforementioned intermediates reveals the capability of the RF/ZFO-based PSF degradation system to generate intermediates with smaller molecular weights, which eloquently explains the system's enhanced photocatalytic degradation and mineralization efficiency due to the synergistic action of multiple active species. Besides, quantitative structure–activity relationship (QSAR) analysis was conducted utilizing the toxicity evaluation software tool (T.E.S.T) to assess the toxicity of TC over the photocatalysis-self-Fenton mechanism based on RF and ZFO and its intermediates according to the following toxicity indices, such as Fathead minnow LC50 (FMLC50), Daphnia magna LC50 (DMLC50), Oral rat LD50 (ORLD50), and Developmental toxicity (DT), as depicted in Fig.S20. The above findings indicate that the toxicity of the low molecular weight substances significantly diminished following degradation through the RF/ZFO photocatalysis-self-Fenton system.

Based on the aforementioned experimental results and analyses, Fig. 5 proposes a potential mechanism for the degradation of antibiotics by the RF/ZFO photocatalysis-self-Fenton system. The RF resin composed of π-conjugated and π-stacked benzenoid–quinoid D-A couples undergoes the separation of photo-generated electrons and holes upon excitation by visible light irradiation [52]. The photo-generated electrons in the conduction band (CB) combine with dissolved oxygen in water to produce the crucial intermediate species
O2 –, which furthers continuing the two-electron reduction of oxygen to in-situ generate H2O2. Furthermore, the in-situ generated H2O2 undergoes Fenton reaction upon contact with ZFO which serving as the H2O2 activator, during this reaction, H2O2 reacts with Fe2+ in ZFO, yielding an abundance of
OH and Fe3+ [53]. Meanwhile, the participation of photo-generated electrons promotes the conversion of Fe3+ to Fe2+, this valence change of the metal significantly aids the efficient utilization of redox-active metals and enhancing the high separation of electron-hole pairs [54]. The cyclic transformation of Fe3+/Fe2+ markedly enhances the photocatalysis-self-Fenton performance for the degradation of TC, simultaneously improving the utilization efficiency of H2O2 and promoting the continuous generation of
OH, and this fortified process intensifies the ability to degrade TC molecules into small molecules such as CO2, H2O, etc.

In summary, a non-homogeneous visible-light-response photocatalysis-self-Fenton system under irradiation, which effectively couples ZFO as H2O2 activator with RF resin as H2O2 producer, has been successfully constructed. By fine-tuning the mass of ZFO within the RF/ZFO photocatalysis-self-Fenton system, promoted photocatalytic degradation performance of antibiotic contaminant with excellent stability. The improved degradation performance can be attributed to several factors: (i) ZFO as an efficient H2O2 activator that can be magnetically recovered and effectively participates in the photocatalysis-self-Fenton reaction, facilitating its progression and yielding satisfactory degradation performance; (ii) compared to a homogeneous Fenton system, the solid photocatalysis-self-Fenton system minimizes the efflux of iron ions. Within the Fenton process, the involvement of photo-generated electrons promotes the conversion of Fe3+ to Fe2+, realizing the efficient cyclical transformation of Fe3+/Fe2; (iii) the established photocatalysis-self-Fenton system allows for efficient utilization of in-situ generated H2O2, which drastically reduces the safety hazards in the production process of H2O2 and is efficiently activated by ZFO to produce an abundance of highly oxidative
OH free radicals, and the synergistic interactions between the
OH and
O2 – radicals contribute substantially to the degradation of antibiotic. This study presents novel insights into the construction of an efficient non-homogeneous RF resins-based photocatalysis-self-Fenton system for antibiotic degradation, offering a new solution for efficient and environmentally friendly wastewater treatment.
